title: Duality of Fluid Mechanics and Solution of Decaying Turbulence
speaker: Alexander Migdal
abstract:
I will describe the duality of incompressible Navier-Stokes fluid dynamics in three dimensions, leading to its reformulation in terms of a one-dimensional momentum loop equation.
The decaying turbulence is a solution of this equation equivalent to a string theory with discrete target space made of regular star polygons and Ising degrees of freedom on the sides.
This string theory is solvable in the turbulent limit, equivalent to the quasiclassical approximation in a nontrivial calculable background. 
As a result, the spectrum of decay indexes is analytically computed, and it agrees very well with real and numerical experiments.
Among the decay indexes there are complex conjugate pairs related to zeros of the Riemann zeta function. The Kolmogorov scaling laws are refuted.
The momentum loop equation does not have finite-time blow-up solutions.

proof_stages.label: Statistical-turbulence problem and loop observable
proof_stages.summary: The talk frames decaying turbulence as the problem of deriving a Gibbs-like statistical theory directly from Navier–Stokes with rough thermal initial data, encoded by a Hopf/Wilson loop average of velocity circulation.

proof_stages.label: Finite-polygon loop equation from Navier–Stokes
proof_stages.summary: To avoid uncontrolled functional derivatives, the loop is replaced by an N-gon and the Navier–Stokes time derivative of its circulation is rewritten, up to controlled 1/N errors, as a finite-dimensional loop PDE whose operator depends only on derivatives with respect to the polygon vertices.

proof_stages.label: Fourier-dual momentum loop equation
proof_stages.summary: Because the loop operator has no explicit coordinate dependence, plane-wave/Fourier modes solve it provided a dual polygon of momenta satisfies an autonomous momentum-loop equation, with the decaying physical branch selected by the boundary condition psi tends to 1 as time tends to infinity.

proof_stages.label: Universal Euler-ensemble fixed trajectory
proof_stages.summary: The decaying ansatz p=(t+t0)^(-1/2)f reduces the dual dynamics to a recurrent equation whose finite-N solutions are rational-angle regular-star-polygon random walks, forming the Euler ensemble proposed as a universal fixed trajectory, with later Q&A emphasizing basin-of-attraction and stability questions.

proof_stages.label: Equal-weight averaging and string-duality heuristic
proof_stages.summary: To turn the Euler-ensemble solution set into turbulence statistics, the talk assumes an equal-weight average over configurations, justified heuristically by analogy with ergodic hypotheses and string/gauge dualities where correlation functions agree without a direct map of variables.

proof_stages.label: Large-N Brownian limit and quasiclassical dual amplitude
proof_stages.summary: In the N to infinity limit the sign random walk becomes Gaussian Brownian motion, giving a one-dimensional dual path integral whose small-viscosity regime is controlled by an explicitly solvable classical saddle and its fluctuations.

proof_stages.label: Number-theoretic observables and decay spectra
proof_stages.summary: Averaging over coprime rational angles leads to totient and zeta-function distributions, from which the speaker derives explicit velocity moment formulae, complex decay exponents, the t^(-5/4) energy decay, and a second-moment scaling function compared with experiment and DNS.

proof_stages.label: Exclusion of finite-time blow-up in the loop equation
proof_stages.summary: For a finite-time singular ansatz in the same momentum-loop equation, positivity forces a zero-vorticity alternative incompatible with blow-up, yielding a no-blow-up result for the finite-noise loop formulation while leaving the N to infinity and zero-noise limits as the remaining Navier–Stokes issue.

examples.name: Gibbs-distribution analogy
examples.note: The speaker compares the missing statistical theory of turbulence to Gibbs statistics derived from Newtonian mechanics, illustrating the kind of object he wants Navier–Stokes to generate.
examples.engagement_reason: He invokes Gibbs distribution as an analogy for the missing statistical theory of turbulence, without doing any calculation or derivation with Gibbs statistics itself.

examples.name: Oscillating-grid decay experiments
examples.note: Physical experiments on grid-generated decaying turbulence are used as evidence that the derived energy decay exponent minus five-fourths is observable.
examples.engagement_reason: He applies his derived decay exponent to the oscillating-grid setup, explains the distance-time interpretation, and compares the resulting five-fourths decay law with the measured experiments.

passes.reason: The oscillating-grid measurements of the t^{-5/4} decay are used to show Kolmogorov-type decay predictions miss the observed exponent.
